danger_signals.visual_form: Sprite-based horror movie enemies (vampires, werewolves, chainsaw maniacs, aliens, giant insects, demonic babies, blobs, mummies)
danger_signals.meaning: Direct threat to player and neighbors; combat encounter required

danger_signals.visual_form: Neighbor sprite removed from level, player loses rescue points
danger_signals.meaning: Failed rescue; stage objective compromised; game-over condition if all neighbors eliminated

danger_signals.visual_form: Gradual screen darkening; visual palette shift from day to night; tourists visibly transform
danger_signals.meaning: World state change: friendly NPCs (tourists) become hostile werewolves; rescue windows close
Imminent player death; loss of life

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy sprites enter aggressive movement patterns when player approaches
danger_signals.meaning: Combat state initiated; threat is active and attacking

safety_signals.visual_form: Distinct NPC sprites (barbecue chefs, teachers, babies, tourists, archeologists, soldiers, dogs, cheerleaders) visible in level
safety_signals.meaning: Objective available; rescue mission active; points obtainable

safety_signals.visual_form: Collectible items (water guns, bazookas, weed-whackers, ice pops, crucifixes, flamethrowers, silverware, etc.) rendered on-screen
safety_signals.meaning: Tactical advantage available; can counter-attack or defend neighbors

safety_signals.visual_form: Health restoration items rendered in level

safety_signals.meaning: Path progression available; level navigation enabled

safety_signals.visual_form: Temporary power-up items (speed boost, monster transformation potions, mystery potions)
safety_signals.meaning: Tactical enhancement; temporary invulnerability or advantage state

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Suburban neighborhoods, shopping malls; civilian safe-zone environments

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Horror movie creature designs; uniform enemy threat aesthetic
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Vampires, werewolves, chainsaw maniacs (replaced with lumberjacks in EU), aliens, UFOs, giant insects, blobs, mummies, demonic babies, evil dolls, squid-men, worms
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Bowels of castle; contaminated suburban areas; pyramids; haunted locations; apocalyptic infestation visuals

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Aggressive alien clones of player characters
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Visual duplicates of Zeke and Julie with hostile behavior

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Bright color palette; normal lighting; visible sun/sky elements; tourists appear as friendly NPCs
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: All neighbors are saveable; tourists do not transform; standard monster spawn rates

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Gradual darkening of screen; palette shifts to darker tones; moon/night sky becomes visible; tourists sprite visibly change appearance
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Tourists transform into werewolves (become unsaveable); monster behavior intensifies; threat level escalates

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Monster sprites distributed throughout level; environmental decay visible; chaotic sprite density increases
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Constant enemy encounters; neighbors at immediate risk; timer pressure (neighbors killed on contact with enemies)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Neighbor sprite disappears from level; points awarded on-screen; visual feedback (text or score pop-up)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Rescued count increases; objective progress tracked; rescues contribute to extra lives

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Empty level of NPC sprites; game-over state visualized
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game loss condition triggered; progression blocked; restart required

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Castle interior environment; Dr. Tongue villain sprite; likely elevated threat visual design
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Final combat sequence; unique boss attack patterns; defeat ends game narrative

environmental_storytelling.example: Suburban neighborhoods establish innocent civilian setting; shopping malls signal consumer culture invasion; pyramids and haunted castles evoke classic horror tropes; castle as Dr. Tongue's lair centralizes villain identity

Barbecue chefs, teachers, babies, tourists, archeologists, soldiers, dogs, cheerleaders represent cross-section of society under threat; each type visually distinct, reinforcing 'neighbors' as relatable everyday people

Vampires, werewolves, chainsaw maniacs (1950s horror references); demonic babies, aliens, giant insects (modern horror/sci-fi); weapon effectiveness tied to horror lore (crucifix vs. vampire, silverware vs. werewolf) reinforces genre authenticity
Unconventional weapons (water guns, ice pops, weed-whackers, soda cans, dishes) signal humorous tone; contrast between silly arsenal and serious monster threat creates tonal duality

environmental_storytelling.technique: Regional censorship as visual identity shift
environmental_storytelling.example: European version: chainsaw maniac replaced with axe-wielding lumberjack; purple blood/gore removed or changed to purple ooze (Nintendo mandate); title shortened from 'Zombies Ate My Neighbors' to 'Zombies'; game-over screen color adjusted (purple US / green EU); these changes signal different regional threat interpretations

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Stationary or passive; saveable; counted as rescue objective
Transform into werewolves; become hostile; cannot be rescued; game treats them as killed
Aggressive pursuit; one-hit kill vulnerability to silverware (lore-based effectiveness)
Aggressive combat; reduced durability against crucifix weapons
Melee-range aggression; regional redesign (NA: chainsaw / EU: axe) indicates censorship response to Texas Chain Saw Massacre reference
Ranged attack (projectile bottle); unusual size-scale threat
Standard monster behavior; vulnerable to cold weapons; 'Monster Frozen Bonus' was incomplete feature
Stationary or wandering; killed instantly on enemy contact; timer pressure (players must reach them before monsters do); no defensive behavior
Temporary invulnerability or monster form; advantage window; limited duration
Unique attack patterns; defeat triggers game ending; positioned as ultimate threat and narrative resolution

notes: Zombies Ate My Neighbors uses horror movie visual homage as primary world-state communicator. The day/night cycle is a critical environmental mechanic that visually transforms friendly NPCs into threats, directly linking visual state to behavioral consequence. Weapon effectiveness is tied to horror lore (crucifix vs. vampire, silverware vs. werewolf), creating a secondary educational layer. Regional censorship (European chainsaw→lumberjack redesign, blood→purple ooze, color palette adjustments) demonstrates how the same game engine communicates different threat levels and cultural values across territories. The game communicates danger through sprite density, monster proximity, and neighbor vulnerability rather than health meters or explicit HUD warnings. Safety is signaled through weapon/potion availability and neighbor presence. The aesthetic tone shifts from humorous (unconventional weapons, cartoonish monster designs) to genuinely challenging (brutal level design, time-pressure mechanics) — this contrast is communicated visually through the juxtaposition of cute neighbor sprites against grotesque monster designs.
